channel.title: flove making-of — nety questions
channel.description: New nety design questions from flove interviews.

item.title: What is the nety 'super' tier for?
item.description:
• Network powers — Super = pooled network stewardship. Direct donations, promote/demote content, govern pools, host/vouch others. Think: civic infrastructure. • Self-sovereignty — Super = run your own full node, host others, independent of the network. Think: personal infrastructure. • Hybrid — Both directions coexist — different features at different sub-tiers of super.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Hybrid

item.title: What shape does the real nety frontend take?
item.description:
• Tabbed app — Like miniappy — single-page with tabs for Access, Identity, MyNet, Circles. Clean but no public landing. • Section stacked — Per spec §8 — sections stacked in one page, pure CSS navigation. Flove-standard approach. • Hybrid — Public section landing (for non-members) + tabbed member app (for logged-in users). Two modes in one file.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Hybrid

item.title: What are the concrete per-tier hardware quotas for nety?
item.description:
• Light (mini/basic) — Minimal bandwidth/storage/compute — read-only or near-read-only. Low cost to run. • Medium (advanced/normal) — Moderate quotas — can contribute content, store some data, run light computations. • Full (super) — Unrestricted — full node, host others, maximum resources. • Defer again — Pin later after the lower tiers work. Keep it abstract for now.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Defer again

item.title: When are identity masks (multiple personae per user) needed?
item.description:
• F2 (early) — Masks from the start — more complex identity model, but privacy-first from day one. • F5 (late) — Masks later — simpler identity first, add pseudonymity when the network has enough users. • Not needed — Skip masks — one identity per user, full transparency. Simpler but less private.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: F5 (late)

item.title: How should crypto key recovery/rotation work for nety identity?
item.description:
• Social recovery — Key recovery via N-of-M social vouchers (friends vouch for you). Standard Web3 approach. • Sidechain — Dedicated key-recovery sidechain — more robust but heavier infrastructure. • Defer — Don't design yet — keep it abstract until the basic identity ladder works.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Defer

item.title: What engagement ranking and anti-gaming mechanics does nety need?
item.description:
• Simple upvote/downvote — Basic engagement signal — easy to game but simple to implement. • Weighted by trust/stage — Higher-stage users have more influence. Anti-gaming via the identity ladder itself. • Reputation score — Composite score from engagement + trust + contribution history. Complex but robust. • Defer — Don't design yet — keep it abstract until the network has users.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Defer

item.title: How should nety curators be selected and held accountable?
item.description:
• Elected by peers — Curators elected by community members. Democratic but slow. • Self-selected + community veto — Anyone can apply, community can veto. Fast but risky. • Tiered appointment — Higher-tier users appoint curators. Efficient but centralized. • Defer — Don't design yet — keep it abstract.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Defer

item.title: How should abuse adjudication work in nety?
item.description:
• Jury system — Random jury of peers decides. Fair but slow. • Curator-only — Curators handle all abuse reports. Fast but centralized. • Hybrid — Curators for urgent, jury for complex. Balanced but more code. • Defer — Don't design yet — keep it abstract.
★ decided: — · ◇ recommended: Defer
